content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

November 4, 1976


Alfred M. Cockreil, Superintendent
Trinidad Union Elementary School
Box C
Trinidad, California 95570

Dear MR. Cockreil:

Assemblyman Keene has provided our agency with a copy of
the resolution adopted by the board of your school district on
September 23, 1975. Essentially, the resolution asks the
Legislature to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974 to exempt
elected school board members from the financial disclosure
provisions of Government Code Sections 87100, et seg.

It is understandable that unpaid school board members
believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose cer-
tain of their private financial interests. However, under
existing law, school districts are governmental agencies;
members of boards of education as well as certain administra-
tive personnel within the school districts "make or participate
in the making of governmental decisions" within the meaning of
the Political Reform Act; and many of their decisions affect
private financial interests. Under these circumstances, the
Act requires that school districts adopt Conflict of Interests
Codes applicable to members and officials who are capable of
affecting such financial interests. Only those financial
interests which could be affected by the filer's official
actions must be disclosed, and it is the school district which
has the first and primary responsibility to identify these
potential conflicts of interest and provide for their disclo-
sure. Hopefully, individuals who object to overly broad dis-
closure will take an active role in the design of each code's
disclosure requirements, so that intrusion of personal privacy
will be kept at a minimum consistent with the objective of
preventing or disclosing conflicts of interest.

The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to
and has provided technical assistance to state and local agencies
in drafting codes. In particular, we have urged agencies to
avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure requirements with a
broad brush but rather to describe with as much precision as
possible the particular types of financial interests which
could be affected by official decisions and which therefore
must be disclosed.


TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5501 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441


Alfred M. Cockrell
November 4, 1976
Page Two

The Political Reform Act was, of course, passed as an
initiative. Section 81012(a) of the Act provides that the Act
may be amended to further its purposes. Among those purposes
are the following:

Assets and income of public officials which
may be materially affected by their official
actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
circumstances the officials should be dis-
qualified from acting in order that conflicts
of interest may be avoided.

Gov. Code § 81002(g).

Given the circumstances, it is doubtful that the exemption of
school board members from the requirements of the Act could be
construed to be in furtherance of the purposes of the Act.

Even assuming, however, that your proposal could be adopted
by the Legislature, and fully recognizing the right of school
board members to express their views, I firmly disagree with
the proposal. School boards make decisions of great consequence
to their communities. Like other public officials, school
board members serve in a position of public trust, and the
people have the right to be assured that their decisions will
be made free from bias caused by their own financial interests.
Disclosure of any financial interests which could foreseeably
be affected by their official actions is a reasonable and
effective means of preventing conflicts of interest. In recent
years the California Supreme Court and the courts of many
other states have ruled that such disclosure requirements are
valid and that any consequent intrusion of the privacy of the
official is justified by the important public interest that is
served.

I hope that this information is helpful. If you have
further questions, please call Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Chief of
our Conflicts of Interest Division, at (916) 322-6464.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

cc: Assemblyman Barry Keene


PLEASE REPLY TO:

SACRAMENTO ADDRESS
STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO 95814
(916) 445-8360

853 "G" STREET
EUREKA 95501
(707) 443-4816

COMMITTEE:
CHAIRMAN, HEALTH
RESOURCES, LAND USE,
AND ENERGY

SUBCOMMITTEE:
HEALTH MANPOWER
MENTAL HEALTH AND
DEVELOPMENTAL DISABILITIES
PARKS AND FORESTRY
FISH AND GAME

Assemblyman

BARRY KEENE

SELECT COMMITTEE:
HEALTH SCIENCES EDUCATION

Representing the
Redwood Empire

JOINT COMMITTEES:
LEGISLATIVE AUDIT ON FEDERAL SOCIAL SECURITY
AMENDMENTS OF 1972

CHAIRMAN
ASSEMBLY HEALTH COMMITTEE

FAIRS ALLOCATION AND
CLASSIFICATION

AND CHAIRMAN

BOARDS:

SPECIAL SUBCOMMITTEE

NATIONAL COMMISSION ON
PUBLIC GENERAL HOSPITALS

ON

ADVISORY HEALTH COUNCIL
WILDLIFE CONSERVATION BOARD

HEALTH CARE INVESTIGATIONS

WESTERN STATES FORESTRY
TASK FORCE

October 29, 1976

Alfred M. Cockrell
Superintendent, Trinidad Union
Elementary School
Box C
Trinidad, California 95570

Dear Mr. Cockrell:

Thank you for sending me a copy of the Resolution of
the Board of Trustees of Trinidad Union School District regarding the requirement that board members file a disclosure
statement under terms of California's Political Reform Act of
1974.

By copy of this letter I am requesting Daniel Lowenstein,
Chairman of the Fair Political Practices Commission to reply
directly to you regarding the possibility that this requirement
can be changed in the near future.

Sincerely,

BARRY KEENE

CC: Daniel H. Lowenstein
     with encl.

# [top portion - partially inverted/unclear text]

[Multiple lines of inverted/unclear text at top]

---

Assortment?

HARRY KEEFER

"Appreciation for
Rehabilitation Center"

CHAIRMAN
ASSEMBLY HEALTH COMMITTEE
DIA CHAIRMAN
"Special Subcommittee
ON
HEALTH CARE INVESTIGATION"

October 28, 1981

---

[Main body text - partially illegible due to image quality]

Dear Ms. Connell:

[Several paragraphs of body text that are difficult to read clearly due to image quality and fading]

Sincerely,

HARRY KEEFER
[illegible]
Assemblyman, 25th District
Long Island

---

[Additional text at bottom appears inverted/unclear]

TRINIDAD UNION ELEMENTARY SCHOOL
Box 9
TRINIDAD, CALIFORNIA 95570

ALFRED M. COCKRELL
DISTRICT SUPERINTENDENT

PHONE
707 - 677-3631

September 1976

CONFLICT OF INTEREST

WHEREAS, public school boards are required by public law (Government
Code Sections 87100 et seq.) to prepare and adopt a district
Conflict of Interest Code, and

WHEREAS, board members of said school boards are required by law to
file a disclosure statement disclosing their interest and
those of their immediate families in investments, real
property, and income, and

WHEREAS, these disclosure statements are by law made available for
public scrutiny, and

WHEREAS, other sections of the Government Code (Section 1090 et seq.)
and Title 2, California Administrative Code (Section 12700
et seq.) and the Education Code (Sections 1174) provide the
needed legal control of board member actions, and

WHEREAS, the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" is an infringement on the rights of private citizens and their immediate families
and is an invasion of personal privacy of board members and their
immediate families, and

WHEREAS, this act can require personal expenses for attorney and
accountant fees by board members holding non-paying elective
board positions, and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of this Code will encourage
private investigative snooping by persons bent on personal
vendettas, and

WHEREAS, compliance with the requirements of the California "Political
Reform Act of 1976" by boards will result in the resignation
of untold numbers of board members throughout the state, and

WHEREAS, school districts and communities of the State need citizens
with financial solvency, business acumen, and personal stability
to serve as board members.


Conflict of Interest                                                Page 2


IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED by the Board of Trustees of the Trinidad
Union School District to publicly state its opposition to the
inclusion of elected public school board members within the re-
quirements of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974" and
it is resolved to urge its legislators to take the necessary
action to bring about the changes needed to exclude school board
members from the requirements of the California "Political Reform
Act of 1974" and it is resolved to publicize this resolution to
all other public school boards in the State of California.

AYES:  Holt, McManus, Sundberg, Susan, Odom

NOES:  None


I hereby certify that the foregoing is a true and correct
transcript of a resolution duly adopted and affirmed by a
formal vote of the members of the Governing Board of the
Trinidad Union School District at a duly constituted regular
meeting of the Board which was open to the public on September
23, 1976, as it appears upon the minutes of the said meeting
of this said Governing Board.


                              [signature]
                              Alfred M. Cockrell
                              Superintendent and Secretary to the Board
